title: Einin
summary: Einin (永仁) was a Japanese era name (年号, nengō; lit. "year name") after Shōō and before Shōan.  This period spanned the years from August 1293 through April 1299. The reigning emperors were Fushimi-tennō (伏見天皇) and Go-Fushimi-tennō (後伏見天皇).
text:
Einin (永仁) was a Japanese era name (年号, nengō; lit. "year name") after Shōō and before Shōan.  This period spanned the years from August 1293 through April 1299. The reigning emperors were Fushimi-tennō (伏見天皇) and Go-Fushimi-tennō (後伏見天皇).

Change of era
1293 Einin gannen (永仁元年): The new era name was created to mark an event or a number of events. The previous era ended and a new one commenced in Shō'ō 6. The era name is derived from the Book of Jin and combines the characters 永 ("eternity") and 仁 ("benevolence").

Events of the Einen era
August 30, 1298 (Einin 6, 22nd day of the 7th month): In the 11th year of Fushimi-tennō's reign (伏見天皇11年), the emperor abdicated; and the succession (senso) was received by his son.
November 17, 1298 (Einin 6, 13th day of the 10th month): Emperor Go-Fushimi is said to have acceded to the throne (sokui) and the nengō was changed to Shōan to mark the beginning of a new emperor's reign.
1299 (Einin 7): The 8th rector of the nunnery at Hokkeji died.
